sections.title: Regular Verb Stems and Conjugation
sections.explanation: Korean verbs always end in -다 in their dictionary form. To conjugate, you remove -다 to get the verb stem, then add the appropriate ending. If the last vowel of the stem is ㅏ or ㅗ (bright vowels), you add -아 endings. For all other vowels (dark vowels), you add -어 endings. For example: 가다 (to go) has stem 가, and since ㅏ is a bright vowel, you get 가 + 아 = 가 (the two ㅏ merge). 먹다 (to eat) has stem 먹, and since ㅓ is a dark vowel, you get 먹 + 어 = 먹어. 하다 (to do) is a special case that always becomes 해 (하 + 여 contracts to 해). This bright/dark vowel harmony rule is the foundation of Korean conjugation.
sections.tip: Remember: ㅏ and ㅗ are 'bright' (양성모음) — they pair with -아. Everything else is 'dark' (음성모음) — they pair with -어. The verb 하다 always contracts to 해, never 하어.

examples.en: to go → stem 가 + 아 → 가 (bright vowel ㅏ merges)
to eat → stem 먹 + 어 → 먹어 (dark vowel ㅓ)
to come → stem 오 + 아 → 와 (ㅗ + ㅏ contracts to ㅘ)
to learn → stem 배우 + 어 → 배워 (ㅜ + ㅓ contracts to ㅝ)

exercises.prompt: What is the informal polite (해요) form of 보다 (to see)?
exercises.explanation: 보다 has stem 보. Since ㅗ is a bright vowel, we add -아: 보 + 아 = 봐 (ㅗ + ㅏ contracts to ㅘ). Adding 요 gives 봐요.

exercises.prompt: Which vowel harmony rule applies to 읽다 (to read)?
exercises.explanation: 읽다 has stem 읽. The last vowel is ㅣ, which is a dark vowel. So we add -어: 읽 + 어 = 읽어.

exercises.prompt: How does 공부하다 (to study) conjugate in the informal polite form?
exercises.explanation: All 하다 verbs follow the special contraction: 하 + 여 contracts to 해. So 공부하다 → 공부해요.

sections.title: 으 Verbs and ㄹ Irregular Verbs
sections.explanation: Verbs with stems ending in 으 (the 'eu' vowel) are special: when you add a vowel ending, the 으 drops out and you look at the vowel before it to determine harmony. If there is no vowel before 으, you add -어. For example: 쓰다 (to write) → 쓰 + 어 → 써 (으 drops, no prior vowel, so -어 is used, and ㅆ doubles). 크다 (to be big) → 크 + 어 → 커. 바쁘다 (to be busy) → 바쁘 + 아 → 바빠 (으 drops, prior vowel is ㅏ, so -아 is used). ㄹ verbs (stems ending in ㄹ) drop the ㄹ before endings starting with ㄴ, ㅂ, or ㅅ. For example: 살다 (to live) → 삽니다 (ㄹ drops before ㅂ), 사는 (ㄹ drops before ㄴ), 사세요 (ㄹ drops before ㅅ). But ㄹ stays before vowel endings: 살아요.
sections.tip: For 으 verbs: just pretend 으 is invisible — look at the vowel before it. If there is no vowel before 으, default to -어. For ㄹ verbs, remember the consonants that delete ㄹ: ㄴ, ㅂ, ㅅ (think: '납세' — taxpaying).

examples.en: to write → 써요 (으 drops, no prior vowel → -어, doubles to 써)
to be busy → 바빠요 (으 drops, prior vowel ㅏ → -아)
to live → ㄹ drops before ㅂ in 삽니다, stays in 살아요
to know → ㄹ drops before ㅂ in 압니다, stays in 알아요

exercises.prompt: What is the 해요 form of 예쁘다 (to be pretty)?
exercises.explanation: 예쁘다 has 으 before -다. The 으 drops, and the prior vowel is ㅖ (dark vowel), so we add -어: 예쁘 → 예뻐요.

exercises.prompt: How does 만들다 (to make) conjugate in the formal polite style (합니다)?
exercises.explanation: 만들다 is a ㄹ verb. Before ㅂ in -ㅂ니다, the ㄹ drops: 만들 → 만드 + ㅂ니다 → 만듭니다.

exercises.prompt: What is the 해요 form of 크다 (to be big)?
exercises.explanation: 크다 has stem 크. The 으 drops, and since there is no prior vowel, we default to -어: 크 → 커요.

sections.title: ㄷ, ㅂ, and ㅅ Irregular Verbs
sections.explanation: Some Korean verb stems change their final consonant when a vowel ending is added. ㄷ irregulars: the final ㄷ changes to ㄹ before a vowel ending. For example: 듣다 (to listen) → 들어요, 걷다 (to walk) → 걸어요. Not all ㄷ verbs are irregular — 닫다 (to close) is regular: 닫아요. ㅂ irregulars: the final ㅂ changes to 우 (or 오 for a few verbs like 돕다) before a vowel ending. For example: 춥다 (to be cold) → 추워요, 어렵다 (to be difficult) → 어려워요, 돕다 (to help) → 도와요. Not all ㅂ verbs are irregular — 잡다 (to catch) is regular: 잡아요. ㅅ irregulars: the final ㅅ drops before a vowel ending. For example: 짓다 (to build) → 지어요, 낫다 (to be better) → 나아요. Not all ㅅ verbs are irregular — 웃다 (to laugh) is regular: 웃어요.
sections.tip: There is no single rule to know which verbs are irregular — you have to memorize them. But a helpful pattern: most adjectives ending in ㅂ are irregular (춥다, 덥다, 어렵다, 쉽다), while most action verbs ending in ㅂ are regular (잡다, 입다, 씹다).

examples.en: to listen → ㄷ changes to ㄹ before vowel ending
to be cold → ㅂ changes to 우 before vowel ending
to build → ㅅ drops before vowel ending
to help → ㅂ changes to 오 (exception among ㅂ irregulars)

exercises.prompt: What is the 해요 form of 걷다 (to walk)?
exercises.explanation: 걷다 is a ㄷ irregular verb. Before a vowel ending, ㄷ changes to ㄹ: 걷 → 걸 + 어요 = 걸어요.

exercises.prompt: 쉽다 (to be easy) is a ㅂ irregular verb. What is its 해요 form?
exercises.explanation: As a ㅂ irregular, the ㅂ changes to 우 before a vowel: 쉽 → 쉬우 + 어 → 쉬워요 (우 + 어 contracts to 워).

exercises.prompt: Which of these ㅂ-ending verbs is REGULAR (does NOT undergo the ㅂ irregular change)?
exercises.explanation: 잡다 (to catch) is regular — it conjugates as 잡아요. Most action verbs ending in ㅂ are regular, while most adjectives ending in ㅂ (춥다, 어렵다, 쉽다) are irregular.
